project_overview.project_name: Productive Social Safety Nets and Youth Employment (PSSNYE) Project
project_overview.project_development_objective: To improve access to social safety nets and income generating opportunities for targeted beneficiaries
project_overview.beneficiaries: Extreme-poor households (15,000 new households plus 6,000 for economic inclusion), youth ages 18-35 (13,000 in public works, 8,000 in entrepreneurship support), with focus on women and persons with disabilities

primary_locations: Five regional headquarter towns (Bo, Kenema, Makeni, Port Loko, Freetown) for youth enterprises
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: partial - Sierra Leone is a post-conflict country with fragility risks including political instability, weak institutions, and vulnerability to shocks

key_components.component_name: SSN Cash Transfers and Provision of Economic Inclusion Support
key_components.description: Provision of quarterly cash transfers to 15,000 extreme-poor households for 3 years; piloting economic inclusion package with start-up capital grants, training, and mentoring to 6,000 extreme-poor households; scaling up emergency cash transfers for disaster response

key_components.component_name: Productive Labor-Intensive Public Works and Life Skills Support for Youth
key_components.description: Sustainable cash for work in rural areas (7,000 youth) and green public works in urban areas (6,000 youth) with life skills training; activities focus on climate resilience, rural road rehabilitation, crop diversification, environmental upgrade, and tree planting

key_components.component_name: Employment and Entrepreneurship Support for Youth
key_components.description: Business grants and training for youth-led household enterprises (8,000 urban youth); establishment of IT platform for youth employment and empowerment opportunities

key_components.component_name: Systems Development, Institutional Strengthening and Project Management Support
key_components.description: Strengthening delivery systems including social registry (SPRINT), electronic payment systems, M&E, GRM, capacity building for implementing agencies, and project management
Zero-allocation provisional component to allow rapid reallocation of funds from other components during large-scale shocks or disasters

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: National Commission for Social Action (NaCSA) as lead implementing agency, with support from: Ministry of Youth Affairs (MoYA), National Youth Commission (NaYCOM), National Social Protection Secretariat (NSPS), Anti-Corruption Commission (ACC), Statistics Sierra Leone (Stats SL), and local councils
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Inter-Agency Forum (IAF) chaired by Vice President serves as Project Steering Committee; Technical Steering Committee (TSC) for technical coordination; District Technical Committees; Community Oversight Committees (COCs) for rural public works
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF; use of STEP for procurement planning; World Bank Standard Bidding Documents for ICB; National Standard Bidding Documents for NCB with World Bank Anti-Corruption Guidelines; RFQ for small procurements; prior review thresholds established based on risk rating
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Results framework with PDO and intermediate indicators; quarterly monitoring through MIS; impact evaluation including baseline/endline surveys; midterm review (March 2025); third-party monitoring; beneficiary satisfaction surveys; GRM tracking; M&E led by NaCSA with support from NSPS and Stats SL

identified_risks.risk_description: General election scheduled for early 2023 during first year of implementation; risks of political interference and governance challenges; potential unforeseen incidents affecting project activities
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Strengthening delivery systems from SSN Project; phased implementation starting with established systems; sensitization materials and staff training on public communication; strict adherence to World Bank procurement regulations

identified_risks.risk_description: COVID-19 pandemic continuing to affect growth outlook; inflation reducing real value of transfers; tight fiscal situation limiting government budget allocation to social protection; large current account deficit
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Annual benchmarking of transfer value against consumption basket with inflation adjustment; government commitment to fiscal discipline and structural reforms under IMF Extended Credit Facility

identified_risks.risk_description: Multiple new interventions across rural and urban areas and different target groups; complexity of implementation within 5-year horizon; risks of unsuitable interventions for specific subgroups; inadequate accountability mechanisms and unclear mandates
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Phased-in design with sequential implementation; building on lessons from previous World Bank operations; technical assistance to implementing agencies; starting small for new activities (EI with 1,000 households); learning and adaptation approach

identified_risks.risk_description: NaCSA lacks experience in some components (youth entrepreneurship, skills training); need for strong collaboration with NaYCOM and MoYA could lead to contradicting mandates; differing capacity levels across implementing agencies
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Dedicated resources for hiring skilled personnel; project management team with senior officials from each MDA; leveraging NSPS, IAF, and TSC for coordination; use of third-party service providers and subcontractors

identified_risks.risk_description: Weaknesses in FM systems with slow improvements; recent fraud allegations involving NaCSA staff in COVID-19 ECT; delays in financial reporting and reconciliations; unreliable IFR reporting; FM performance downgraded to Moderately Unsatisfactory in December 2021
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: ACC monitoring of targeting, enrollment, and payments; enhancing capacity of MoF Internal Audit Directorate; regular reconciliations between systems and payment service providers; dedicated FM staff; quarterly FM reviews by World Bank; enhanced internal audit with quality assurance; external audit by acceptable auditors

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk of COVID-19 spreading to staff and beneficiaries; unscheduled lockdowns and closures affecting implementation; security risks from crowd management during emergency response; large volumes of personal data collection with potential for abuse
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Adherence to health safety protocols; security engagement plan/protocols for crowd management; data governance mechanisms incorporating international best practices; data minimization, accuracy, use limitations, retention policies; informing data subjects and allowing corrections

identified_risks.risk_description: Potential elite capture and exclusion of vulnerable groups in beneficiary selection; exclusion of women and persons with disabilities from LIPW activities; temporary land occupation and potential displacement; SEA/SH risks; OHS risks; child labor and forced labor risks; GBV risks including IPV
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Robust beneficiary selection criteria with community participation; transparent communication of eligibility criteria; strong outreach to women and persons with disabilities; flexible working hours and childcare support; code of conduct for project staff; GBV Action Plan; secure cash transfer mechanisms; gender-sensitive beneficiary selection; enhanced GRM with survivor-centered approach; safety measures in public works; voluntary land donation procedures with World Bank approval

triggered_safeguard_policies: Environmental and Social Standards (ESS) 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS 3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS 5: Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement
ESS 6: Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Leveraging existing GRM from SSN Project managed by ACC with toll-free number (515), call center, web-based complaints management system, community monitors, and referral pathways. Enhanced to address GBV complaints using survivor-centered approach with confidentiality protections and referrals to service providers
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Project addresses three key gender gaps: (1) female-owned enterprises suffering more from COVID-19 through EI package with business grants and training; (2) female youth constrained by domestic responsibilities through childcare support in rural public works; (3) female youth lower financial inclusion through e-payment pilot with digital financial literacy training. Female recipients prioritized for cash transfers (85% target); at least 60% of EI beneficiaries female-led; at least 50% of public works beneficiaries female; 60% of youth enterprise beneficiaries female. GBV prevention measures including secure transfer mechanisms, women leaders' participation, behavior change messages, safety measures in public works, and GRM adaptations
vulnerable_groups: Persons with disabilities (target 30% of SSN beneficiaries, 15% of public works)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project designed with conflict sensitivity considerations: (1) Post-conflict context with fragility risks including political instability, weak institutions, and youth frustration as potential source of fragility; (2) Youth bulge (75% of population below age 35) with limited employment opportunities identified as source of fragility; (3) Project aims to address youth frustration through employment and income opportunities; (4) Transparent beneficiary selection and strong community participation to prevent elite capture and tensions; (5) ACC monitoring and GRM to ensure accountability and prevent grievances from escalating; (6) Life skills training including conflict resolution; (7) Phased implementation to allow learning and reduce implementation risks that could undermine social cohesion
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Sierra Leone is a post-conflict country (civil war ended 2002) with multiple fragility factors: (1) High poverty (56.8% using national poverty line, 43% using international line) with limited progress in recent years; (2) Economic growth not translating to job creation - structural transformation incomplete with 55% employment in agriculture; (3) High youth unemployment and NEET rates (18% overall, 31% for youth with disabilities, 19% for female youth); (4) Low human capital outcomes (36% of Human Capital Index); (5) Recurring shocks including EVD (2014), economic crisis from commodity price collapse, floods/landslides (2015, 2017), COVID-19; (6) Climate change increasing vulnerability with 24th highest natural disaster risk globally; (7) Food insecurity rising from 48% to 63% between January-June 2020; (8) Weak institutional capacity and governance challenges; (9) High rent-seeking and corruption risks
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security risks identified and mitigation measures: (1) Crowd management risks during emergency response and cash distribution - security engagement plan/protocols to be adopted; (2) COVID-19 spreading risks requiring health safety protocols; (3) Road safety concerns in feeder road rehabilitation works; (4) Physical safety of women in public works requiring separate facilities, adequate lighting, limited distances in isolated areas; (5) SEA/SH risks from power differentials between beneficiaries and project actors - code of conduct, secure distribution mechanisms, female staff participation; (6) Data security risks from large volumes of personal data - data governance mechanisms with international best practices
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Low risk of displacement with mitigation measures: (1) Small-scale subprojects unlikely to cause displacement; (2) Experiences from YESP and Ebola LIPW projects show communities can design subprojects without displacement; (3) Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) prepared; (4) Voluntary land donations anticipated for Component 2 with World Bank approval required; (5) Land use agreements to temporarily grant access to community/private land without permanent appropriation; (6) Screening criteria in ESMF to exclude activities causing displacement; (7) Site-specific ESMPs and RAPs to be prepared when subproject details identified; (8) No project-financed activities expected to require involuntary resettlement
